title: Walt Disney: America's Great Storyteller
text:
Hello. Today on the rest is history, the
story of the most important single
individual of the 20th century. And that
person is of course Walt Disney. We'll
be looking at his rags to riches story
from inventor to triumphant cultural
figure. We'll be looking at the great
films Snow White, Fantasia, and Mary
Poppins. And we will be asking, what
does Disney mean for American history,
for American culture, for world culture?
Why has Disney been so successful? And
yes, Tom Holland will be singing.
[music]
This episode is brought to you by Hive.
You know, history is full of
transformations. The Romans shifted from
republic to empire. The Tudtor
transformed monasteries into country
houses. And you know what? Hive has had
one of its own. Everybody knows Hive for
smart thermostats. But now they've
evolved into something much greater.
Solar panels that help you to power your
home with clean energy. Heat pumps that
pull warmth from thin air. EV chargers
that charge your car while you're
asleep. All managed through a single
app. This is all about giving people the
power to transform their homes from
waste to efficiency, from dependence to
control, from consumption to
contribution. So history's next great
transformation may not be happening in
parliaments or palaces. It may be
happening in your home. A quiet
revolution minus guillotines. Hive. Know
your power. Visit hivehome.com
to find out more. Subject to survey and
suitability. Hive app. Compatible with
selected heat pumps.
>> [music]
>> When you wish upon a star, makes no
difference who you are. Anything your
heart desires
will come to you.
If your heart is in your dream,
no request is too extreme.
When you wish upon a star, as dreamers
do.
>> So, if you're still with us, that was
When You Wish Upon a Star, which is of
course the official company anthem of
Disney. Now, Disney, which effectively
began as a a young man tinkering
um basically pursuing a hobby, is now a
business worth $200 billion. Everybody
knows the name of Disney. It's the owner
of Star Wars franchise, of Marvel, of
course, of theme parks everywhere on the
planet, most famously Disneyland and
Disney World in America. It is an
organization that has an almost
unparalleled grip on the world's
imagination, particularly of children,
which is when the imagination is most
formative. Now, Tom, you were very keen
to kick off with this song. Partly
because you were desperate to do those
vocals cuz you love any opportunity to
sing on the show for some reason, but
also I think because the song captures
something of the nostalgia and the
sentimentality that is at the core of
Disney. I don't mean those things
projoratively because of course they're
tremendously appealing for a lot of
people, aren't they?
>> Yeah, it's the great anthem of Disney
and it's a reminder as well that Disney
has its origins in the kind of the
golden age of Hollywood, but more
specifically it's a reminder of the
company's origin in the dreams and the
distinctive personality of the man that
you just mentioned, the filmmaker who
who founded Disney, gave it his name. Um
the figure who probably more than any
other I would say embodies American
culture in the 20th century and that is
of course Walt Disney.
>> Oh definitely. He's one of the most
important cultural figures globally of
the 20th century I would say.
>> Yeah absolutely. And so the lyrics of
when you wish upon a star which you read
rather than sang but you you read it
very beautifully. Anything your heart
desires will come to you. I mean that,
[laughter] you know, that may seem very
schmaltzy to people, but I would argue
that it is up there with Rock Around the
Clock, so the Bill Haley classic that
really kind of ushered in rock and roll
as a song that marks a key turning point
in the culture of 20th century America.
>> Totally. I think anybody who's tempted
to listen to the show and say, "Oh,
Disney, why are they doing Disney? It's
just a thing for children." I think
completely misses the impact that Disney
has had in shaping the imaginations and
therefore the cultural political choices
of hundreds of millions of people ever
since Disney really broke through in the
what the 1930s I suppose.
>> Yeah. And that's that that song I mean
it it's the first song in history to
have been sung by a talking insect for
instance. So that's definitely a first.
I mean obviously it's not literally sung
by a talking insect. It was sung by an
actor called Cliff Edwards and he was
ventriloquising a cartoon character
called Jiminy Cricut in a film called
Pinocchio. And I'm sure lots of people
will have seen Pinocchio, but those who
haven't. Pinocchio is a a puppet, a
puppet of a boy who's carved by a toy
maker called Japetto. And Japetto, as
he's going to sleep, wishes upon a star,
wishes that this puppet he's made will
come to life. And sure enough, a blue
fairy appears and brings Pinocchio to
life. And Jiminy is the cricket that's
appointed by the blue fairy to serve
Pinocchio as his conscience. And over
the course of the film, they have all
kinds of adventures. So Pinocchio um he
ends up basically enslaved to a
villainous puppeteer who locks him up in
a cage. They visit Pleasure Island.
Dominic, you love a pleasure island.
>> I do like Pleasure Island. a very
sinister theme park um where Pinocchio
narrowly avoids being turned into a
donkey and they get swallowed up by a
whale. And I think we are so used to
animation bringing stories like this to
life today that it can actually be
incredibly hard to recapture the sense
of stupaction
of absolute wonder that greeted a film
like Pinocchio. I mean nothing, you
know, just jaw-dropping for people to
see this.
>> Yeah. And Disney, Walt Disney, even if
he'd never made a single theme park or
indeed if he'd done nothing after the
Second World War at all, if he'd, you
know, been knocked down by a bus or
something, he would still have a very
high place in the kind of cultural
annals of the last century because he
more than anybody is the person who
establishes animation as a mass media
art form, isn't he?
>> Yeah. So, basically his career up to
Pinocchio is a series of massive
cultural landmarks. So 1928 you have
Steamboat Willie or Rat on a boat as
Theo calls it which introduces Mickey
Mouse to a mass audience and it's
crucial in the history of animation and
indeed of film because it's the first
cartoon with fully synchronized sound.
Sound that is coming directly um from
the characters so from Mickey and so on.
Uh and it's kind of it's the jazz singer
of animation really. It's the one that
fuses animation and audio. Then five
years later, 1933, you have a cartoon
called Three Little Pigs. And I mean,
again, kind of amazing innovations in
that. So the color perhaps particularly
very vibrant, very true to life. Yeah.
People's minds are absolutely blown by
this. They've never seen anything like
it. But also the characterization, the
fact that the these are animated
characters who are not just kind of
tools for a gag or something. They they
seem to have distinct personalities. you
know, the three little pigs, they will
have their various characteristics. And
of course, anticipating When You Wish
Upon a Star, it has a classic theme
song, Who's Afraid of the Big Bad Wolf?
And this absolutely bores itself into
the consciousness of depression era
America. Um, and to quote Neil Gabler,
whose definitive biography of Disney,
kind of drawing on all kinds of
previously um untapped sources by
biographers, we're going to be referring
to a lot over the course of this
episode. He describes that song as it
indisputably became the nation's new
anthem. Its cheerful whoop hurled in the
face of hard times.
>> Yeah. And then you've got I mean the
biggest film I would argue Disney's
supreme cinematic achievement is Snow
White. That's 1937 I think and that's
the first fulllength animated feature,
isn't it? And up to that point the idea
of doing animated feature at all just
seemed a impossible and almost you know
mindboggling as sort of it would be such
an extraordinary enterprise so
timeconuming so expensive that it had
never occurred to anybody to do it.
Yeah. And so the tradition is that
people are so gobsmacked when they learn
what Disney's planning that they call it
Disney's folly. Um but you know it's
it's all very Japetto. Disney's clearly
wished upon a star because when it's
released, it's a massive global hit. In
fact, I mean, at one point, I was
astonished to read this. It was the most
successful sound film ever released and
it's a huge box office hit then, but
it's also a critical hit. So, in 1944 in
a wartorrn Britain, the great British
film director Michael Powell, as in
Powell and Pressburgger, sums up why
Snow White and Seven Dwarfves is a
cultural landmark. So he said in that
film Disney abolished naturalism,
established stylistic settings and
backgrounds, controlled his design of
color and sound, a feat not yet in the
power of any other producer and held
audiences in raptured all over the
world. So you can see there that it's
obviously absolutely massively
influential on animation but also on
other films as well. I mean you can see
it in pal and presber's films.
>> You look at the other films of 1937 and
1938. I'm just looking at the list now.
Saratoga, May Time, The Good Earth, uh
1938, Boytown, Alexander's Ragtime Band,
Test Pilot, You Can't Take It With You,
Sweethearts. Nobody watches those films.
Nobody's even heard of most of those
films. I guess the vast majority Snow
White and the Seven Dwarfs. Even if
you've never seen it, you're familiar.
You still got it in your head. Yeah,
it's absolutely living in your head.
>> It's a massive success. And Pinocchio is
the follow-up. It's the second feature
length film that that Disney makes. And
I guess it kind of sets, you know, the
seal on his reputation as the most
innovative filmmaker of the age. And so
when you wish upon a star, which sounds
very very syrupy to us, it kind of
serves as the anthem for everything that
makes him cutting edge. I mean, and
that's the amazing thing about it. You
know, it goes on to win the Oscar for
for best film in 1941. It's really
important to emphasize how mad that is.
An animated character, an animated
insect winning an Oscar. I think again
and again when you look at Disney,
you've got to think what is it like for
people to listen or to see this for the
first time. And I think that one of the
issues with getting a proper sense of
Disney's cultural importance is that
he's really the first great example of,
you know, what becomes a classic 20th
century American phenomenon. He's an
innovator whose influence is so profound
that today you tend to take it for
granted. So on the cultural level, you
know, he's a massive innovator. But
we've been describing that he's been he
develops he shapes an entire new art
form. And I guess that the closest
parallel in the second half of the 20th
century to that would be Elvis um you
know who who in a similar way makes a
kind of an emergent genre his own and
then broadcasts it to the world. Um but
it's Disney who gets there first. So to
quote Neil Gabler again, it's Disney who
helped establish American popular
culture as the dominant culture in the
world. Well, in the next episode, we'll
be describing Nikita Krischoff and his
wife and how they loved Disney films.
Nikita Kristoff's wife had first seen
them in the 1940s. And the fact that
somebody in the Soviet Union has fallen
in love with Disney's vision, a very
American vision, gives you some sense of
the kind of global power that it had
even then.
>> But I think part of what Chris very
reluctantly has fallen in love with is
not just the cultural kind of resonance
of Disney. It's also the technological
one. So again and again when I was
reading Gabbler's biography I was
reminded not just of of you know Elvis
or someone like that a kind of cultural
innovator but of Steve Jobs who was a
great technological innovator. So if you
think of Disney and and and Steve Jobs
both of them are complete perfectionists
absolutely kind of obsessed by the
opportunities that they've been given by
a technology that's still in its
infancy. Um both of them are very
Californian figures and both of them
start off kind of tinkering uh in Steve
Jobs case famously in a shed but Disney
you know his early animations are made
in garages and so on and both of them
end up becoming the public face of you
know a vast and wealthy company and I
would say that in the 21st century
[snorts]
Apple and Disney they're globe spanning
beer moths who for most people across
the world are part of the public face of
of America.
>> Totally. Actually, Steve Jobs ended up
on the board of Disney, didn't he? After
he sold Pixar to Disney. So, um it's a
nice comparison. All right. But Walt
Disney's own career. He's he's born in
December 1901. And in some ways, he's a
19 I think he's a quite 19th century
figure. You know, many years ago, I
wrote about Samuel Smiles, the great
self-help uh guru of the Victorian
period, who wrote a book called The
Lives of the Engineers. And he was
Samuel Smiles was fascinated by all
these people who um you know they they
started kind of tinkering the James Watt
and Matthew Bolton and kind of steam
engine people and people like that and
Walt Disney always feels to me like one
of those kinds of figures. There is
something very backwardlooking about
him. I think very nostalgic. He's
somebody who would completely have been
at home 20 or 30 years earlier. I think
a classically American version of that.
And actually, if you look at his
ancestry, it's like it's almost kind of
comically
it contains within it so many different
threads of history. So, I mean,
amazingly, I wasn't expecting to to
bring up the Norman conquest in an
episode on Walt Disney, but Disney
claimed, and I gather that
genealogologists say it's not a
completely mad idea, but he was
descended from a Norman who came over
from the the Norman town of Isnney. So
Disney is so in that case his forebears
you know were were Norman aristocrats
but they um they they seem to have
migrated from England to Ireland and
then from Ireland in 1834 to the new
world and Disney's grandfather this guy
called brilliant name Keell Keell Disney
I mean basically he engages in
everything you would expect a prospector
in you know 19th century America to do.
So, he works as a farmer. Uh, he works
as a gold prospector. He drills for oil.
Um, none of these ventures really pays
off for him. He's a bit of a drifter. He
ends up in Florida and there his son
Elias meets a 16-year-old girl called
Flora. And Flora, again, brilliant. You
know, she's the kind of living
embodiment of American mythology. So,
she's the descendant of a pioneer who
traveled to America in 1636.
So she's almost within touching distance
of the pilgrim fathers. And Elias and
Flora marry on Neo's day 1888 in
Florida. But Elias is like his dad. He
is never a man for staying in one place.
And in 1890 he upsticks. He moves from
Florida and he and his wife um head off
to Chicago. And in Chicago he works as a
carpenter. Um, and it is in Chicago in
the dead of winter in late 1901
that Walt Disney is born.
Oh, that was nice. So, um, his childhood
is quite Denzian, isn't it? Cuz his
father is pretty tough. Neil Gabler says
he's a this sort of harddriven devout.
He worked hard, lived modestly, and
worshiped devoutly. But he's also moving
around the whole time because he never
really settles, never makes a success of
anything. The most obvious place where
they live is in Missouri, smalltown
Missouri, a place that becomes
absolutely embedded in Walt Disney's
imagination and in you could argue in
the world's imagination because it's the
inspiration for the main street that you
find in Disney's parks. And this is a
place called Marceline in Missouri. If
you anyone's seen the or heard the
Philip Glass opera about Walt Disney,
it's all about him trying to get back to
Marceline and his memories of Marceline
and he has this sort of romantic
fantasy, doesn't he, of smalltown
America.
>> But for Elias, it's actually pretty
tough cuz he's trying to scratch a
living as a farmer and he fails. So in
1911, he moves to Kansas City and he
buys a newspaper delivery route, as
Americans would put it, a route. Um, and
essentially this means, you know, he has
to get up very early in the morning and
shove newspapers through people's letter
boxes. And he gets Walt and his elder
brother Roy to help him. And Walt by
this point is 9 years old. So he's
having to get up at 4:30, you know, even
when it's winter, absolutely freezing in
Kansas City in the dead of winter, you
know, wading through thick snow drifts.
Um, and unsurprisingly, I mean, it
affects his schoolwork. He's too often
too tired to concentrate. So that is
quite Denzian. I think there is an
element of of the blacking factory about
that and it doesn't go brilliantly well.
But the whole time Elias has been saving
up money and in 1917 he moves back to
Chicago and he invests his savings in a
jelly factory. Do Americans call it
jello? I think they do, don't they?
>> I think when they call it jelly they're
talking about jam. But when they're
talking about jello, they're talking
about jelly. Is it jello or is it is is
it jelly or jam?
>> It's jello. Come on, make your minds up,
Americans. Sort yourselves out. But it's
not all grim, right? It's not all. So,
there is actual genuine jelly um amid
the custard. I don't know what where I'm
going with that metaphor. Um cuz there's
light as well as dark in childhood.
>> Well, yeah, cuz so um Elias is a very
hard man. We've said that. But his
mother Flora is very gentle, very
nurturing, and the key role that she
plays in uh Walt's development is that
she encourages him with his great
obsession which has become drawing um
and specifically cartoons.
And reading about Walter as a child, you
get this sense of him as being
simultaneously dreamy and obsessional.
And I guess that those are
characteristics that, you know, will
accompany him throughout his life. And
there's a famous Tom Sawyer style
episode where Walt draws cartoons in
creassote all over a white fence. Um,
and even Flora is cross with him about
that. But in general, she's very
supportive. And another very supportive
figure in the family is his elder
brother Roy, who um is very practical,
very hard-headed. He gets a job in a
bank that enables him to kind of buy
Walt toys that otherwise Walt wouldn't
have. Um, and I think he in a way is
kind of he's kind of more of a a father
figure perhaps even than Elias is, you
know, he's always looking out for for
Walt. So there is security amid all the
kind of the uncertainty, the constant
moving. And I think also the other thing
that the the constant moving does is
that it does give him a very precocious
feel for America. You've mentioned how
Marceline will kind of haunt his
imagining, but I think it also he you
know he been Kansas City, he's been to
Chicago, he has a sense of of what makes
America tick and that again will be kind
of crucial to his ability to give form
to the fantasies and dreams of America.
Yeah, he's uh Kansas City has given him
a sense of the big city, but he's also
got a sense of the small town. He's got
a sense of the landscapes. He's been on
trains, you know, all of that kind of
world, that Midwestern world is kind of
um has taken root, hasn't it? Has put
down roots in his imagination.
>> Yeah. And so he's uh you know, by the
time he's 16, he's he's got his great
passion, he's got his drawing. Um he's
got an absolute work ethic. I mean,
Elias has absolutely kind of you know,
instilled him with that. And he's, you
know, he's not an intellectual. He's not
got a brilliant education, but he does
have a very broad range of experiences
of memories, you know, of dreams
basically.
>> Mhm.
>> And then in uh September 1918, of
course, the First World War has been
raging. America has finally decided to
join in the fun. He has one final
adventure before adulthood claims him.
He enlists for service with the Red
Cross Ambulance Corps in France. And
he's so young when he does so that he
has to lie about his age on his um
enlistment form. And actually by the
time that he and his valet volunteers
reach France, they have to go to La
Harra because um the port they're
originally going to is full of um uh
bombed ships. The war is actually over.
But you know, he's going into a
shattered France. He sees scenes of
devastation and death everywhere. And
undisputably this broadens his horizon
massively as does his introduction to
you know the architecture of Europe. So
he's stationed at one point near
Versailles at another point near the
Louvra. He he um is billeted in a town
called Nefetto which gives him a feel
for the architecture of the old you know
the old continent. Um and all the time
he's continuing to draw his cartoons and
he's turning his hands to making props.
And in a way, what he's doing there is
he's improving reality. So with one of
his friends, he pillages German helmets
from local rubbish dumps and he scuffs
them up in dirt. He shoots bullet holes
through them and then he sells them as
souvenirs from the front to tourists. So
again, you [laughter] can see elements
of the Disney story.
>> Definite hint of Disneyland about that.
So he goes back to Kansas City uh in the
autumn of 1919, doesn't he? So he's not
on the Western Front for a very long
time, but he's changed quite a lot. He's
a much bigger, heavier person.
>> Hello, Mom. I mean, you can imagine all
that.
>> He's taken up smoking and he smokes all
his life, doesn't he?
>> He has a terrible hacking cough.
>> And he's much more self-confident. He's
much more, you know, by like so many
people, he's gone to the war and he's
he's come back a man.
>> Yeah. He's not going to he's he's not
going to do what his father wants, which
is to invest his career in jello. He's
turning his back on the jello. He he
wants to follow his dreams. And
obviously his dream is to draw cartoons.
>> But Walt being walt, a man who is never
content with what he's got, the scope of
this ambition becomes steadily ever more
sweeping. So from you know just drawing
static cartoons he moves into animation
and then from animation into the
production of animation and from
production into something you know I
mean the kind of the dream of an
ambitious young man in 1920s America. He
sets up a studio in Hollywood and Walt
had moved to Los Angeles in July 1923.
Partly because obviously it's the center
of the the movie business, but partly
also because his brother Roy, who had
also been serving in the First World War
and had actually seen action, he's there
and he is recuperating from TB in um in
a hospital. and Roy, you know, he's the
practical Disney as opposed to the
visionary Disney, which is Walt. You
know, they form a good team. And the
studio that they set up is called the
Disney Brothers Studio. But I think
there's never really any doubt where the
center of gravity in that that business
is. And sure enough, by 1926,
when they obtained their own studio lot
on Hyperion Avenue in Los Angeles, the
name is no longer Disney Brothers. It is
now Walt Disney. And just on Hollywood,
Hollywood has only been going really
since the 19 the mid 1910s. So Hollywood
in itself and indeed the city of Los
Angeles of which it's a part is a kind
of great experiment. It's a great
innovation and everybody there has come
from somewhere else as Walt has. So he's
part of this incredible generation of
people who are you know creating
something new.
>> Yeah.
>> All the time.
>> Yeah. And I think that just as kind of,
you know, Los Angeles has pulled itself
up from its bootstraps, so has Walt. I
mean, that's what makes him a absolute
paradigm of a guy living out the
American dream. And throughout the
1920s, while he's trying to to make his
way, you know, he he's in terrible
poverty. He's basically subsisting on
baked beans. You know, there are times
where he can only afford a weekly
shower. And there there are times where
he looks so thin that, you know, people
who are concerned about him worry that
he's got TB as well. like his his his
brother Roy. So he really, you know,
he's living on the absolute edge in the
early years of his career, but by 1928,
he's looking, you know, he's looking
pretty established. So he's got his
studio, which is named after himself,
and he's got growing numbers of
animators who are working with him. And
Disney has a real eye for brilliance in
animation. He's married, so his wife
Lillian um married her in 1925.
And he has a breakout cartoon character
called Oswald the Lucky Rabbit. And
Oswald is notable both for very phallic
ears, which are always kind of they're
kind of either slumping or very
tumescent, but also Oswald and this is a
kind of prefiguring of what will happen
with the three little pigs and Mickey
Mouse. He he's a kind of character.
>> Yeah. He's got personality.
>> Yeah. He's not just a a vehicle for
visual gags. Um, and this makes him, you
know, this makes him a very very popular
figure. But there is an absolute
disaster because the Disney brothers are
are very naive in business. Even Roy and
they've got embroiled with an absolute
shy in New York.
>> Charles Mintz, his name is Mr. Mintz.
>> Yeah. Very sinister. And he, first of
all, he poaches um the the Disney
animators. And then the absolute
bombshell, he reveals they don't
actually own the intellectual property
rights to Oswald. Their distributor
does. Um and Walt is massively in debt.
He can't afford to keep them. So
basically Oswald the uh you know this um
phallic rabbit, he's lavished all his
time, all his money, all his creativity
on it. And now he finds he doesn't even
own it. And it's an absolutely
devastating moment that might have
destroyed a lesser man. But it doesn't
destroy Walt. And he vows two things.
Firstly, he is never again going to lose
control of his characters and his
animations. They are always going to
remain his. And secondly, he is
determined to create a new character who
will be bigger and better than Oswald
had ever been. And this is Mickey Mouse.
And actually, the birth of Mickey Mouse
is one of those moments. It's a bit like
JK Rowling on the train coming up with
the idea of Harry Potter because
actually there's a train in this, isn't
there? The claim is that Disney has gone
to New York and he's discovered that Mr.
Mintz has stolen his animators and has
stolen Oswald and he's getting the train
back to Los Angeles. Very long journey
and it's on the train that he comes up
with the idea for Mickey Mouse and he
sketches the character. But I think
you're going to say, aren't you, Tom,
that this isn't true? I mean, Mickey
Mouse, you could say, is the most famous
movie star of the 20th century, and so
it's not surprising that there are lots
of myths that surround him, you know, a
bit like King Arthur, who um Disney will
go on to make a a film about. So, yeah,
the story on the train, you know, that
he he's or that he's been keeping mice
um as pets while he was in Kansas City.
There are all kinds of stories, and I
think it's impossible to know the truth
about it. Um, what does seem to be true
is that initially Walter been planning
to call this mouse he's he's got in his
imaginings uh Mortimer and Lillian his
wife says that Mortimer's too city and
so that's how they come up with Mickey.
>> I think Mortimer Mouse would have been
even more successful.
>> I don't know. I'm not sure about that.
You know, whatever the truth, the the
fact is that Mickey has this breakout
role in Steamboat Willie, the the jazz
singer of animation. And this sets Walt
on one of the great creative spikes in
the history of Hollywood, in fact, in
the whole history of of American
culture. And key to this is the fact
that Disney's development of animation,
we've said how it's culturally
innovative, how it's technologically
innovative, it is also commercially
innovative. And by the early 30s, Mickey
is not just an American phenomenon, he
is an international phenomenon. And for
the first time, you start to get
European intellectuals
um infusing over Disney. So there's a
German critic who describes Mickey as a
preeminent personality of the screen
today as the only artist who exemplifies
in his work and technique the pure form
of talking films. And because of this
